audit_items.proof_trace: Four parsing functions — str2strval (L1542), DCE2strval (L1644), IA52strval (L1729), and quotedIA52strval (L1823) — call LDAP_STRNDUPX (ber_strndup_x) in their escapes==0 branch without checking the return value for NULL; on allocation failure they return 0 (success) with val->bv_val=NULL and non-zero val->bv_len. This NULL bv_val propagates into an LDAPAVA la_value field, and when the DN is later serialized via strval2strlen (L2060-2061), the code computes end=NULL+len-1 and then dereferences p[0] on a NULL pointer. The NONPRINTABLE branch in the same if-block has a correct NULL check, making the omission in the printable path an oversight rather than intentional design.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker sends LDAP requests with DN attribute values composed of long printable ASCII characters (no escaping required, the common case), driving the server into memory pressure via heap exhaustion; when LDAP_STRNDUPX returns NULL the parser silently succeeds with a NULL bv_val, and any subsequent DN serialization call (e.g., via ldap_dn2str or ldap_rdn2str) dereferences the NULL pointer in strval2strlen, crashing the LDAP server process.
